tossups.question: In a novel titled for this type of character, a writer narrates his father’s resistance during the Dirty War after finding articles about the janitor Alberto José Burdisso’s murder. At the end of a novel, a girl leaps “into the void” after some of these characters invite her to the Big Midnight Feast. That novel titled for these characters is set on New Year’s Eve at an apartment complex construction site and is by César Aira (“SAY-sar EYE-rah”). These characters’ murmurs fill a town at “the very mouth of hell” in a novel in which the hostess Eduviges Dyada (“ay-doo-VEE-hess dee-YAH-duh”), the cook Damiana Cisneros, and the deaf mule-driver Abundio Martínez are revealed to be this type of character. Juan Preciado visits a town inhabited by these characters in Pedro Páramo. For 10 points, Clara del Valle (“VAH-yay”) can communicate with what beings in an Isabel Allende (“ah-YEN-day”) novel partly titled for them?
tossups.question_sanitized: In a novel titled for this type of character, a writer narrates his father's resistance during the Dirty War after finding articles about the janitor Alberto Jose Burdisso's murder. At the end of a novel, a girl leaps "into the void" after some of these characters invite her to the Big Midnight Feast. That novel titled for these characters is set on New Year's Eve at an apartment complex construction site and is by Cesar Aira ("SAY-sar EYE-rah"). These characters' murmurs fill a town at "the very mouth of hell" in a novel in which the hostess Eduviges Dyada ("ay-doo-VEE-hess dee-YAH-duh"), the cook Damiana Cisneros, and the deaf mule-driver Abundio Martinez are revealed to be this type of character. Juan Preciado visits a town inhabited by these characters in Pedro Paramo. For 10 points, Clara del Valle ("VAH-yay") can communicate with what beings in an Isabel Allende ("ah-YEN-day") novel partly titled for them?
tossups.answer: ghosts [or fantasmas; or dead people; or spirits; or specters; accept The House of the Spirits or La casa de los espíritus; accept My Father’s Ghost Is Climbing in the Rain or El espíritu de mis padres sigue subiendo en la lluvia] (My Father’s Ghost is Climbing in the Rain is by Patricio Pron. Pedro Páramo is by Juan Rulfo.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: ghosts [or fantasmas; or dead people; or spirits; or specters; accept The House of the Spirits or La casa de los espiritus; accept My Father's Ghost Is Climbing in the Rain or El espiritu de mis padres sigue subiendo en la lluvia] (My Father's Ghost is Climbing in the Rain is by Patricio Pron. Pedro Paramo is by Juan Rulfo.)

tossups.question: Gabriel García Márquez has said that he felt blocked as a novelist until reading this man. This man’s novel The Golden Cockerel was published in 1980. twenty years after it was originally written. One story by this author is about a father taking his (*) wounded adult son Ignacio to see a doctor. That story, Can’t You Hear the Dogs Barking, was included along with Tell Them Not to Kill Me in this author’s collection “The Plain in Flames”. This author’s most famous novel is about Juan’s promise to his dying mother and his journey to visit his father in the town of Colama. That novel by this author was described by Gabriel Garcia Marquez as a life-changing discovery. For 10 points, name this Mexican author of Pedro Páramo.
tossups.question_sanitized: Gabriel Garcia Marquez has said that he felt blocked as a novelist until reading this man. This man's novel The Golden Cockerel was published in 1980. twenty years after it was originally written. One story by this author is about a father taking his (*) wounded adult son Ignacio to see a doctor. That story, Can't You Hear the Dogs Barking, was included along with Tell Them Not to Kill Me in this author's collection "The Plain in Flames". This author's most famous novel is about Juan's promise to his dying mother and his journey to visit his father in the town of Colama. That novel by this author was described by Gabriel Garcia Marquez as a life-changing discovery. For 10 points, name this Mexican author of Pedro Paramo.

tossups.question: Remarkably, four separate instances of these events happened to author Antonio di Benedetto during his imprisonment under the Videla regime. In one novel, a former teacher organizes one of these events with a scarecrow after making arbitrary decrees such as animals out after six becoming public property. One of these events occurs at the end of a story whose protagonist habitually hides in the hills after hacking a (*) landowner to death with a machete. A father addresses his son Justino (“hoo-STEE-no”) before one of these events in that story from The Burning Plain. A character who is said to have survived “fourteen attempts on his life, seventy-three ambushes,” and one of these events spends it thinking about the time he saw a diamond-like object at a tinkers’ camp. For 10 points, Colonel Aureliano Buendía remembers seeing ice with his father while facing death by what method?
tossups.answer: firing squads [accept mock firing squad; prompt on executions or mock executions] (The Juan Rulfo story is “Tell Them Not to Kill Me!”)

packet.name: 02 - Foster et al, Thompson et al

tossups.answer_sanitized: firing squads [accept mock firing squad; prompt on executions or mock executions] (The Juan Rulfo story is "Tell Them Not to Kill Me!")

tossups.question: A collection by this author that was first translated into English by Georg D. Schade includes a story in which the narrator and his sick brother’s wife Natalia arrange the brother’s death during a pilgrimage to a shrine at the title place. The narrator of one of this author’s short stories worries that Tacha will end up a whore like her two sisters after a cow is washed away by a flood. Thirty-five years after a dispute over cattle pasturing caused the main character of one of this man’s stories to kill Don Lupe Terreros, the arrival of a firing squad prompts that character to make the title plea to his son Justino. This author of “Talpa”, “Because We Are So Poor” and “Tell Them Not to Kill Me!” wrote a novel adored by Gabriel Garcia Marquez in which ghosts describe the title character’s influence on the town of Comala to his son, Juan Preciado. The collection The Burning Plain was written by, for 10 points, which Mexican author of Pedro Paramo?
tossups.answer: Juan Rulfo [or Juan Nepomuceno Carlos Perez Rulfo Vizcaino] 2
tossups.answer_sanitized: Juan Rulfo [or Juan Nepomuceno Carlos Perez Rulfo Vizcaino] 2

bonuses.leadin: An author with this first name wrote a pioneering magical realist novel in which time stops as Hurtado and Julia escape from the bloodthirsty General Rosas. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: An author with this first name wrote a pioneering magical realist novel in which time stops as Hurtado and Julia escape from the bloodthirsty General Rosas. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this first name, shared by the author of Memories of the Future and an author with the surname Poniatowska who wrote an oral history of a massacre of Mexican students in The Night of Tlatelolco.
Elena Poniatowska wrote a biography of this Mexican author, who was also the husband of Elena Garro. This author wrote The Labyrinth of Solitude.

The protagonists of a short story by Elena Garro wake up one day transformed into these animals. A man carries his wounded son, the bandit Ignacio, in a short story titled for a sound made by these animals.
parts_sanitized: Give this first name, shared by the author of Memories of the Future and an author with the surname Poniatowska who wrote an oral history of a massacre of Mexican students in The Night of Tlatelolco.
Elena Poniatowska wrote a biography of this Mexican author, who was also the husband of Elena Garro. This author wrote The Labyrinth of Solitude.

The protagonists of a short story by Elena Garro wake up one day transformed into these animals. A man carries his wounded son, the bandit Ignacio, in a short story titled for a sound made by these animals.
answers: dogs [accept perros] (The Garro story is “The day we were dogs,” and the second story is Juan Rulfo’s“Don’t you hear the dogs barking?”)
answers_sanitized: Elena [accept Elena Poniatowska; accept Elena Garro; accept Helene Poniatowska]
Octavio Paz
dogs [accept perros] (The Garro story is "The day we were dogs," and the second story is Juan Rulfo's"Don't you hear the dogs barking?")

bonuses.leadin: In this story, a foreman and an administrator are among a group of people executed in a mock bullfighting game. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this story that ends as the main character meets his son after spending years in jail and working to derail a train. This is the title story of a collection that also includes “North Pass” and “They Have Given Us the Land.”

“The Burning Plain” begins with the epigraph “Now they killed [one of these animals] / But [her offspring] remain,” taken from a popular ballad. Demetrio Macías joins a revolution after government soldiers kill one of these animals in a novel whose 1929 English translation is titled for a word derived from the name of these animals.
The Burning Plain and The Underdogs both depict the bleakness of the 1910s revolution in this country, which is the birthplace of both Juan Rulfo and Mariano Azuela.
answers: “The Burning Plain” [or “El Llano en llamas”; accept “The Plain in Flames” or “The Llano in Flames”] (by Juan Rulfo)
dogs [or perros or perras or perritos; accept The Underdogs] (The Underdogs is the English title of Mariano Azuela’s Los de abajo.)
answers_sanitized: "The Burning Plain" [or "El Llano en llamas"; accept "The Plain in Flames" or "The Llano in Flames"] (by Juan Rulfo)
dogs [or perros or perras or perritos; accept The Underdogs] (The Underdogs is the English title of Mariano Azuela's Los de abajo.)
Mexico [or United Mexican States; or Estados Unidos Mexicanos]

bonuses.leadin: In a story from Sergio Pitol’s (“SAIR-hee-oh pee-TOLL’s”) collection Mephisto’s Waltz, this animal gives the narrator a message of “twelve enlightening words.” For 10 points each:
parts: A novel by a different author opens with a man’s description of a young woman from the Carpathians painting what type of animal at a zoo?
Pitol’s story “The Panther” is included in Sun, Stone, and Shadows, an anthology of stories from this country. This country is home to the author of the poem “Sunstone.”

Sun, Stone, and Shadows also includes this author’s story “Tell Them Not to Kill Me!”, which is part of his collection The Burning Plain. In a novel by this author, the title tyrant lets the town die of hunger out of spite while he is mourning Susana’s death.
answers: panthers [accept black panthers; prompt on big cats or felines or Cat People; prompt on black leopards] (The novel is Manuel Puig’s Kiss of the Spider Woman.)
Mexico [or United Mexican States; or Estados Unidos Mexicanos; reject “Estados Unidos”] (Octavio Paz wrote “Sunstone.”)
Juan Rulfo [or Juan Nepomuceno Carlos Pérez Rulfo Vizcaíno; prompt on Vizcaíno] (The novel is Pedro Páramo.)

bonuses.leadin: Foreign-language answer acceptable. A book named after one of these places includes the story “It’s Because We’re So Poor,” which is about a family who loses a cow in a flood. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this type of place that titles a collection containing the stories “They Gave Us the Land” and “Tell Them Not to Kill Me.” A character whose last name is a word for this type of place loves Susana San Juan.
This author of The Burning Plain wrote about Juan Preciado’s time in the ghost-inhabited town of Comala in his novel Pedro Páramo.

This Colombian author allegedly read a gifted copy of Pedro Páramo twice in one night and claimed that he could recite it forwards and backwards. Pedro Páramo influenced his novel One Hundred Years of Solitude.

Juan Rulfo [or Juan Nepomuceno Carlos Pérez Rulfo Vizcaíno]

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, answer the following about magic realism writers:
parts: This Uruguayan author wrote La tregua in the style of the personal diary of the protagonist, Martín Santomé. His El cumpleaños de Juan Ángel is a novel written in verse, while this author explored other forms of poetry in his Rincón de Haikus.

This Colombian novelist wrote about a veteran of the Thousand Days’ War who still hopes to receive the pension he was promised some fifteen years earlier for his service in the army of Aureliano Buendía, in No One Writes to the Colonel.

This Mexican writer explored magic realism in his collection The Burning Plain and other Stories. In his novel Pedro Páramo, this author wrote about a man named Juan Preciado who travels to his recently deceased mother’s hometown to find his father, only to come across a town populated by spectral figures.
answers: Gabriel García Márquez [or Gabo, or Gabito, prompt on García]
answers_sanitized: Mario Benedetti
Gabriel Garcia Marquez [or Gabo, or Gabito, prompt on Garcia]
Juan Rulfo

parts_sanitized: This Uruguayan author wrote La tregua in the style of the personal diary of the protagonist, Martin Santome. His El cumpleanos de Juan Angel is a novel written in verse, while this author explored other forms of poetry in his Rincon de Haikus.

This Mexican writer explored magic realism in his collection The Burning Plain and other Stories. In his novel Pedro Paramo, this author wrote about a man named Juan Preciado who travels to his recently deceased mother's hometown to find his father, only to come across a town populated by spectral figures.

bonuses.leadin: Mario Vargas Llosa’s Nobel acceptance speech uses several examples of scenes in which literature can provoke the same shudder in people of all faiths. For 10 points each:
parts: The first example is when the title character of this novel swallows arsenic. It is the subject of Vargas Llosa’s book The Perpetual Orgy.
The final example is when the reader of this novel realizes that all of the residents of Comala are in fact dead.
answers_sanitized: Madame Bovary
Pedro Paramo [by Juan Rulfo]

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about some geographically-related novels, for 10 points each.
parts: The title character’s desire to lure Susana San Juan back to the town of Comala drives much of the backstory of this novel, whose first half is narrated by Juan Preciado.
The yet-untranslated Federico en su Balcon was the last novel published by this author, who fictionalized the disappearance of Ambrose Bierce in The Old Gringo and wrote The Death of Artemio Cruz.
This author made his name by writing novels of the Mexican Revolution, most prominently a tale of Luis Cervantes and Demetrio Macías called Los de Abajo, or The Underdogs.
answers_sanitized: Pedro Paramo [by Juan Rulfo]
Carlos Fuentes Macias
Mariano Azuela Gonzalez
